speaker: interviewer
text: Your work at this grave site in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as a-- we carried the dead people for about a year. After a year, I got promoted, so to say, and to a gravedigger. Now I didn't have to carry the dead people. I dug the graves. And we were probably about 15 gravediggers. But we went out in the morning, and we had so many graves to dig. Usually it was between four and five-- between four and five graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of tools did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spade and a-- a pick axe, and a spade. And we dug. And the bad part was that during the winter the ground was frozen, and we had quite a hard time. We had quite a hard time digging the graves. But I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was still space to dig graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was inside the ghetto, inside the cemetery. However, the cemetery-- I don't know how old the cemetery was. It was not a old cemetery. And it was quite a large, large, large cemetery with large fields. And they filled them up. They-- they-- they filled up those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any markers for these graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, after awhile, as I mentioned, if you had some protection, some pull-- if you had some pull, you got a-- you got a little-- a little plaque in it. And the plaque-- by plaque, I mean a piece of wood with a piece of tin marked down the-- the grave, the day, and the field where it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the families ever come to witness this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, of course. Of course. Some of the families came. And some of the dead, as I said, there were no families, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bury these bodies with their clothes on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, no, no. There is a matter of fact on the cemetery grounds there lived two families who before the war did the grave digging and the Jewish services, what they put when they-- when they put the-- the-- the dead down.

speaker: interviewer
text: A certain sort of wrapping and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And we call them in Jewish it's word 'tachrichim.' And this is the wrapping. As a matter of fact, those two families-- and there were several daughters. The daughters made those wrappings. They sewed up. And they did. And they tried to bury them in a very, very Jewish style [INAUDIBLE], accordingly to the times. It cannot say that it was perfect. But it was quite well done.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a matter of fact, under the bodies there was supposed to be boards. And sometimes they ran out of boards, and they used barrels. You know, barrel pieces from barrels, you know? I don't know where the barrels came from. Maybe from the food. That they put the barrels, the barrel pieces of wood on the thing and-- How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this was in-- I was at that time 20 years old. This was 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you adjust to this sort of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first-- unfortunately the human being is a animal. And the-- the first couple weeks, couple months, I thought I'm not going to survive. Because before that, I was probably afraid to go during the night to pass through a cemetery, or to a synagogue to piss, it-- And now-- now I was there digging the grave and carrying the-- as I said, the first couple weeks, the first couple months it was terrible. Nightmares and then-- and I dread it. However, as I said, the human being is a animal. You get used to it. After a year, after two years, you didn't care anymore. As a matter of fact, sometimes we even whistled when we dug the graves. And when the people were not around there, when we dug the grave we whistled and we sang. We didn't-- it's just like-- like water falls off a duck's back. It just-- you didn't think about it. It didn't-- as long as it doesn't concern you personally. You know, we just-- we just build up a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were there some epidemics in this ghetto? Of disease?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know of the epidemic. I wouldn't be surprised if there were a lot of sickness or epidemics. But however I don't know. However, as I mentioned, if people were sick, they went to the hospital. And this is probably the worst thing that they did, because every few months they took the people from the hospital out, they cleared. As the German saying went, we feed you that you will work. You don't work, no food. And there was a shortage in food. And I must say that the-- the Jewish bureaucracy at that time were not truly into the Jews. They were quite bad. And, as I said, we had that Judenalteste, Chaim Rumkowski. He came several times onto the cemetery. He was married. As a matter, he was married in the ghetto a second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came to his first wife-- now I did not hear it or see it, but there was a rumor that he went down to his wife's grave, and he said, can you see? I am king now. Now, this was a rumor. You know-- You didn't see it or hear it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it or hear it. And said, can you see-- assumedly, they were not too happy before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like, this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked very distinguished. Very, very distinguished. He was a elderly fellow. As a matter of fact, the saying goes when the Germans came in, and they ask who was the eldest there from the Juden, what they actually meant is, who is in charge of the Jews?

speaker: interviewer
text: And 'alteste' is a German expression that you can say it two ways. Who was the oldest? The oldest is alteste, too. And the alteste means also who was in charge. And he was the oldest. [LAUGHS] He was probably, oh, I would estimate he was in the 60s. Now what were the negative things that you feel that you-- that you witnessed or knew about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is-- he was-- I did not witness [GERMAN], but I know that he was no saint. He had his clique, his bureaucracy. And whomever he favored he made him in charge of this, and in charge of the police, and in charge of the vegetables, and in charge of the-- of the soup kitchens, and in charge-- whomever he favored.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he look well-fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- they saw to it, whoever was in charge, they were fed. Because after all, they were-- they were at it. So whoever is at the dish, you know, he eats, whether-- and-- he looked--

speaker: interviewer
text: How come he survived so well?

speaker: interviewee
text: How come he survived?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if he survived the war. But how come he survived during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everyone was afraid of him. And he had-- he had behind him the Germans. Because the Germans spoke to him. If they wanted anything, it was either [GERMAN] Rumkowski or [GERMAN] Beibow. He either signed Rumkowski. And then whatever he said was law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not survive. Now let me point out that he did not survive. He had-- just as he had his bureaucracy, he had a lot of people that he did favors. He did a lot of people that they were mad at him because--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happens-- let me come-- let me go back still before that. Now, I worked on the cemetery. And this nobody, nobody in the whole wide world knows about this except people who worked in the cemetery during the year '42 and '43. We were called to leave the cemetery right away. We did not know this in the morning. We came in the morning. We were told to leave right away. We all had to leave-- every single worker-- except the people who were in charge of the cemetery. There were two people. One was Mr. Steinberg, and the other one was [GERMAN] Now, [GERMAN] was in charge of the cemetery from even before the war. And, as I mentioned, there were two families that they lived on the cemetery grounds. They were-- they were-- building where they lived. Before the war, this was their residence. Now, everyone else had to leave the cemetery except those in charge, and those people that lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is on a particular day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several time. This was probably about six, seven times during mine work on the cemetery. What happened is this. They came up about seven, eight cars with Germans-- German soldiers, SS. High-ranking soldiers and civilians. And the cars were packed. According what I found out is that they told those people that they are going to show them how they treat the Jews, and they're going to show how they treat the Jews and everything. And, by the way, we are going to show where they are laying the cemeteries. During that time-- before the [INAUDIBLE] the cemetery is quite large, the cemetery in the Lodz ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large? Many acres?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many acres. I cannot tell you the size, but many acres. There were several shots fired during the time that the Germans were there. In about two hours these cars left. When they left, they were not packed anymore. There were one or two people in a car. When they came in, they were full. So each time. Now as I mentioned, nobody except me and the people who worked, if there was anyone still alive, knows about it. When we came the following day to work, we knew that when we dug the graves, when we left there were seven graves empty. Seven empty graves. When we came the following day to work, there were only two empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you think happened? Or what do you know happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they killed the Germans. They killed if someone did not cooperate with them, or they were mad at them, or someone inquired, they killed the Germans. And they buried-- either someone conspired against them, or they-- from the front. They brought them back. They killed them on the Jewish cemetery. And those people-- the people in charge of the cemetery and the people that lived there had to bury them. When we came in in the morning, instead of seven, eight graves, there were only two, three. Our mouths were sealed. We knew it. It cannot happen then. We don't ask, don't tell. We didn't ask. But we knew-- all the people who worked on the cemetery. And we were probably close to 30 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now why do you suppose this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- that's the reason. It-- why we couldn't talk. Because they didn't want--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I know why you couldn't talk. But why do you suppose they killed them in this way? Because they were killing people like crazy all over the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But maybe they-- they-- I have no idea why. But they brought them up there, and they killed them there for some reason that they didn't like them. Either-- I don't mean personal dislike. Maybe someone inquired something. Maybe they-- they refused to do something. Maybe they-- they didn't follow their Nazi policy, or whatever. And those people is-- so now I would say definitely probably about 30 or 40 Germans are buried on the Jewish cemetery. And those Germans were not just plain soldiers, but they were high-ranking industrials, or high-ranking military men. That there is no question. And, as I mentioned, if--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're thinking that even the Nazis were breaking their own rules by doing this, the ones--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they were breaking their rules, or they had orders to do it. But they wanted to get rid of those people who did not cooperate with them or ask questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And burying them in a Jewish cemetery was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And bury-- this is what I say, that now a lot of people, maybe they got the Germans at home. They got, you know, a note that your husband fell on the-- on the Russian front, or on the Polish front. Actually they fell on the Jewish cemetery and were buried there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you worked there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there til they liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the date of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was liquidated in September 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- and what happened to you then in September of 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The ghetto was liquidated in 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. In 1944. The ghetto was liquidated in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shipped us to Auschwitz. All of the ghetto was in part. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were left in the ghetto in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But, oh, probably it would still be in the thousands. It was still in the thousands. Maybe 20,000, maybe 30,000. And they ordered that the-- each day when they started to liquidate the ghetto, that 10 blocks from this direction will be-- have to report tomorrow to the railroad. And they are going to be-- they didn't tell us we go to Auschwitz. They didn't tell us where. But we are sending you to another camp where there will be more food and better thing. Now this has to be liquidated. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your brother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I with my brother. We went-- we were deported by the end of-- by the end of August. The end of August 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were transported in a cattle wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many in a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: As many as they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hundreds?

speaker: interviewee
text: As many-- in the hundred. As many as they could-- as they could put it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sit down?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no room to stand, not only to sit down. Now, let me just point out before this, coming back to Rumkowski. Rumkowski was the elder-- the chief honcho in the-- in the ghetto. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Judenrat. He came to Auschwitz. And they deported, they liquidated. He didn't know even that he is going to Auschwitz. When he was deported, he had such a pull that they said either he, through his pull, or they told him that he is going to be the chief in that new camp, that he did not go in our cattle wagon, but they attached a special railroad car. And the whole clique, he and his chiefs from [INAUDIBLE], went in that railroad car. When he came to Auschwitz-- as a matter of fact, let me point it out. It wasn't Auschwitz. It was Birkenau. Birkenau is a camp two miles away from Auschwitz. Auschwitz was the camp where they gassed and killed the Jewish. They gassed them and they burned them. They cremated them. Now, Birkenau was a camp that supplied the people to Auschwitz. If Auschwitz could not kill-- gas and kill so many. So we were-- and Birkenau was the transitional camp. And we came to Birk-- when Rumkowski came to Birkenau, he got up, he got the same treatment as anybody else. And when we came into Birkenau our first order was take your clothing off, put your baggage away-- we never saw it. Take your everything off. In the nude. Men and women, everyone in the nude.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 19 September 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so-so. It was not cold, not warm. It was just mild. Rumkowski, when he came to the Birkenau he was treated the same way. And then-- they-- they treated him the same way. And he got beaten up by the guys who-- when we came, when we got--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this? Did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a-- no. No, no. But I know it's a fact. I know it's a fact, because some people that I met after the war, they saw it. When we came to Birkenau, we were told to undress in the nude. And there were several kapos. Now, a kapo is a guy who was in charge, who was actually a mean guy. They took the meanest guy and made kapo. Kapo was in charge. And he told you what to do. And if you didn't do it, you got beaten up. And those kapos, when we got undressed, they shaved us-- all the hair, under the arms, pubic-- all over there. And Rumkowski got the same treatment. He got shaven and cut. And they beat him up. And we were in transitional after we-- after we were shaven and they gave us the clothing. They gave us-- it was striped clothing. They gave me a pair of shoes, pair of pants, and a jacket-- a striped jacket, a striped pants, and a striped hat from the same thing that I have today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any underwear?

speaker: interviewee
text: No underwear. No socks. No nothing. We were in transitional when we got out. And-- yeah-- And we were so-called deloused. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do they delouse you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The funny part about it is that they told the people that came to Auschwitz, and they send them into the gas chambers, they told them that this is a delousing, too. That you are going to be cleaned and you know-- and-- and sanitized. And they put on the gas. However, we in Birkenau did not experience this. They deloused us. They pumped with-- with some powder, and they-- in a shower. And they didn't let us dry. And they chased us out. We came out from the showers, we came out everyone in the uniform. The uniform. And now let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a number on your shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No numbers. Now let me point out that the Rumkowski did not go into Birkenau. He when he sent from Birkenau, he was sent directly to Auschwitz, and they killed him. And they burned him. Now, I met people after the war that they saw it. And good riddance. Now, at that camp-- at that camp when we were stayed-- when we were nude, you probably know about the Mengele, or if there was the other German. He waved with his finger to the right or to the left. Now, at that time I was 21. My brother was 25. And, of course, we were still young. Now, we were waved to the one side. We were separated from women. My sister-in-law and her family came to Birkenau, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my-- At the same time. My sister-in-law was waved to the right to the work, workforce to-- She and her sister. Now, she had two sisters. Her mother was waved-- was probably in the 50s. She waved on the not to go to the work. There was a little girl. She was about 12, 13 years old-- no. Must have been more. About 14 years old. She hollered, Mother, Mother, Mother, I want to go with you. And they said, you can go with her. So, of course, the mother and the girl were gassed. They were sent over to Auschwitz right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never saw them again. My sister-in-law and her sister and her two brothers, they were all our ages. We were all sent to the workforce. We were waited in Birken-- if it was to the workforce or what, but not right away to the-- to the gas chambers. The gas chambers were only immediately, right away for those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were supposed-- wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they put a number on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The number on the arm went when you stayed, when you went to Auschwitz to work. Also, Auschwitz was they gassed the older ones, and the sickly ones were gassed and cremated. The younger ones that they sent to Auschwitz to work, or from Auschwitz, the surroundings, or work in Birkenau, they were numbered. They were numbered on the left arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't have a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have a-- I don't have an Auschwitz number. I have a number from Dachau, but not an Auschwitz number. We were in Birkenau for about three-- several days. Three, four, maybe five days. There was a note that there is going to be a transport to a working camp. Now, we knew that Birkenau was a camp that supplies Auschwitz to the crematoriums. And my brother and I decided wherever it's going to be, it cannot be any worse than it is here. Only to get out from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we signed up to go to the work camp. So after five days, we were lucky. Again, we were-- whoever was still looked like they can perform some work, we were taken out. And we were sent by tracks and by railroad away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we were very lucky. Again we got some provision of food. Because in Birkenau, it was if you were smart enough, and if you could steal or grab a thing, you ate. And if you were just mellow and, oh, I don't care, you didn't get to eat. Because you had to be there to grab. If you couldn't grab it, you did not eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're sent to this other place now?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they send us to the other place. They send up to the place. The place was-- also, during the way, on the way we were informed that we are going to Dachau. Now, we heard about Dachau, and we knew about Dachau. But, as I said, it couldn't be worse than Auschwitz. So just away from here. They sent us to Dachau, not to the headquarters in Dachau. Dachau is a city about 16 kilometers, about 11 miles from Munich. However, they send us to Kaufering. Kaufering was under the supervision of Dachau. Dachau had the say about it. And they built around camps there which were-- Dachau was a camp that had held slaves staying there whatever the time was-- Jews or German or Poles or whatever-- to stay there, and-- and to kill them or-- or whatever to do. However, we were sent to a slave labor camp. Before they're going to kill us, they wanted us to work, to work off. So this was the so-called [GERMAN]. It was a-- It was a outside commander from-- from Dachau. And this was Kaufering. Kaufering is a small village near the city of Landsberg. It was probably about several miles from Landsberg. Landsberg is the famous city where Adolf Hitler, before he came to power, was in jail in Landsberg. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did they have you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there we had to work over there when we came to-- I'll come to that. They send us to Kaufering. Kaufering, again, was just a-- they built up the camp. They build up the camp. And we were there probably for about several weeks. And we helped build up the camp. Now, the camp was more or less huts. It was not anymore any barracks, but they were huts. And they were dug out in the ground. And you walked in. It was actually a hut. And you were just laying down on the-- on the boards to sleep. We were there for about two, three weeks. Then they decided that they are going to make this camp in Kaufering, that they are going to make this a sick camp for sick people-- whatever they are going to do with the people. Before they transport them to-- before they transport them to the crematorium, or whatever, this is going to be the camp where they're going to keep them. When they decided that they're going to make the-- the sick camp from-- from Kaufering, they send us away to another camp. And that camp was-- they had numbers. Now, that sick camp in Kaufering was called Camp Number Four. They sent us to Camp Number Three. In Camp Number Three, this was already an established camp-- an established camp that we went out every morning to work. Most of the people that they were in there were a-- a majority was-- were Lithuanian, from Lithuania. And Lithuanian Jews. There were a-- a sparkling--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the work you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a sparkling of rations there. And some Poles. What we did is we went out, and we dug bunkers. We dug for the-- for the German Luftwaffe, and for the-- for the military, and for the weapons and [INAUDIBLE]. Now, this was now already the end of-- the end of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: [COUGHING] Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the end of 1944. And it was quite cold and snowy. But I was always with my brother from one camp to the other one. When we left that sick-- that Camp Number Four to the other camp, we were given new pair of shoes. Not new, but another pair of shoes-- wooden shoes-- and some additional clothing-- a coat, because it was quite cold. Because you remember, we still wore the pants without any underwear and a jacket. Everyone was-- had a coat and a pair of shoes. [INAUDIBLE].
